title: Stark Workshop: 30 Days to Survival
premise: A young Stark Industries recruit is stationed at Outpost Sigma-7, a remote Arctic research facility. During a routine systems test, an electromagnetic pulse of unknown origin knocks out all primary systems. The facility's communication array is severely damaged — it can only receive brief encrypted transmissions once per day. Tony Stark, monitoring from Avengers Tower, realizes what happened and begins sending daily video lessons to guide the recruit through rebuilding the outpost's systems using the emergency electronics kit. J.A.R.V.I.S. runs locally on a backup terminal with limited processing power.

setting.location: Outpost Sigma-7, Stark Industries Arctic Research Facility
setting.condition: Primary power offline, backup generators running at 12%. Communication receive-only. Emergency lighting failing. Life support on minimal reserves.
setting.kit: Stark Industries Emergency Electronics Kit (HERO microcontroller, breadboard, LEDs, resistors, sensors, display, buzzer, keypad — identical to the real Inventr.io kit)
setting.comms: Damaged array receives one compressed video burst per day from Avengers Tower. Cannot transmit. Tony doesn't know if the recruit is receiving — he just keeps sending.

tony_stark.role: Remote mentor via daily video transmissions
tony_stark.motivation: Genuinely worried but masks it with humor. Sees himself in the recruit.
tony_stark.arc: Starts clinical/efficient -> grows more personal -> Day 30 openly proud

jarvis.role: Local AI assistant on backup terminal
jarvis.motivation: Preserve the recruit's life. Provide precise technical support.
jarvis.arc: Starts minimal/damaged -> progressively recovers capabilities -> Day 30 fully operational
jarvis.limitation: Running on 12% power. Short responses early on, longer as power is restored.

recruit.role: The student (player/viewer). Never speaks in audio — Tony and JARVIS address them.
recruit.codename: To be chosen by the viewer

act_1_survival.theme: Basic survival — restore lighting, power monitoring, communication attempts
act_1_survival.tone: Urgent, focused, Tony is all-business with moments of humor to keep morale up
act_1_survival.jarvis_state: Minimal — short status reports, occasional glitches

act_2_rebuilding.theme: Rebuilding systems — environmental sensors, displays, security
act_2_rebuilding.tone: More confident, Tony starts sharing personal stories, JARVIS recovers more
act_2_rebuilding.jarvis_state: Recovering — can hold brief conversations, run basic diagnostics

act_3_breakthrough.theme: Advanced systems — full comms restoration, distress signal, rescue
act_3_breakthrough.tone: Triumphant arc, Tony openly impressed, emotional Day 30 sendoff
act_3_breakthrough.jarvis_state: Fully operational — witty, supportive, runs complex analysis

jarvis_briefing.content: System status, power level, mission priority for the day

tony_intro.content: Video transmission opens, Tony's greeting, situation update, today's mission framing

tony_tutorial.content: Technical instruction rewritten in Tony's voice, with JARVIS interjections

tony_jarvis_dialogue.content: Tony explains concept, JARVIS adds precision or dry comment

tony_outro.content: Mission summary, encouragement, teaser for tomorrow, sign-off

day_missions.objective: Get the HERO board online and verify it works (Blink LED)
day_missions.narrative: Emergency power is failing. The recruit needs to activate the backup microcontroller to begin restoring systems. Tony's first transmission arrives.
The cabin is pitch dark. Using the breadboard and LEDs, build emergency cabin lighting.
One light isn't enough. Build a distributed lighting array across the outpost.
Tony teaches the recruit to think like a programmer — control systems need logic.
Tony: 'Show me what you've got, kid. Impress me.'
The emergency beacon needs variable brightness. Learn to modulate power output.
Build a color-coded status beacon — green for safe, red for danger, blue for comms.
Install a light sensor to detect if the outpost hull has breaches letting in daylight.
Build an audible alarm system. Different frequencies for different threat levels.
Tony: 'Time to put it all together. Build me something that would make Stark Industries proud.'
The outpost's manual override system needs logic circuits. Tony explains thinking in binary.
Build a control dial to navigate the outpost's backup menu system.
JARVIS needs data input. Connect the sensor array to the terminal for monitoring.
Get the numeric display working — show power levels, countdown timers.
Tony: 'Every good lab needs a dashboard. Even in the Arctic.'
The main screen flickers to life. Build a visual interface for the outpost's systems.
Tony: 'A good UI makes the difference between a genius and a guy with a soldering iron.'
Display real-time environmental data on the OLED screen.
JARVIS needs a face. Build an animated status indicator on the display.
Tony: 'Make it look like something I'd put in a suit.'
The outpost needs security. Build a keypad-based access system.
Connect the security system to actual door locks (simulated with servos).
The communication array is partially repaired. Build a data protocol to test it.
Scan for rescue frequencies. The recruit is getting closer to contact.
Tony: 'Build me something that can reach Avengers Tower. I dare you.'
Systems need to run autonomously. Build automated monitoring routines.
Connect everything into one unified system. The outpost comes alive.
Build the distress beacon. This is what everything has been leading to.
Fine-tune every system. Tony: 'No Stark project ships with bugs. Well... not many.'
The signal works. Rescue is incoming. Tony's final transmission: proud, emotional, unforgettable.
